title: GeodeticOps.jl
text:
Features
GeodeticOps.jl is a pure-Julia package for geodetic computations on Earth’s ellipsoid.

title: GeodeticOps.jl
text:
7 coordinate types with automatic conversions: LLA, LLARad, ECEF, ENU, NED, AER, UTM
Geodesic distances: Haversine (great-circle) and Vincenty (ellipsoidal)
Forward and inverse geodesic problems via Vincenty’s formulae
Geodesic interpolation: intermediate points and midpoints along geodesics
Polygon area on the ellipsoid using spherical excess
Bounding boxes: axis-aligned and distance-based
Multiple datums: WGS84 (default), GRS80, Clarke 1866, WGS72

title: GeodeticOps.jl
text:
Installation
using Pkg
Pkg.add("GeodeticOps")

title: GeodeticOps.jl
text:
Quick Example

using GeodeticOps

nyc = LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)
london = LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074)

d = distance(nyc, london)
println("NYC -> London: $(round(d / 1000, digits=1)) km")
println("Bearing:       $(round(bearing(nyc, london), digits=1))deg")
println("Midpoint:      $(midpoint(nyc, london))")

NYC -> London: 5585.2 km
Bearing:       51.2deg
Midpoint:      LLA(lon=-41.27790142901274°, lat=52.3894744727656°, alt=0.0m)


All operations default to WGS84. Pass datum=GRS80 (or any Datum) to override, or set DEFAULT_DATUM[] = GRS80 to change the global default.







Figure 1: Geodesic path (blue) versus rhumb line (dashed gray) from New York City to London. The geodesic curves northward because it follows the shortest path on the ellipsoid.

title: Getting Started
text:
Creating Coordinates
This tutorial walks through the core features of GeodeticOps.jl.
The most common coordinate type is LLA (longitude, latitude, altitude):
# New York City (altitude defaults to 0)
nyc = LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)

LLA(lon=-74.006°, lat=40.7128°, alt=0.0m)
# Mount Everest summit
everest = LLA(86.925, 27.988, 8848.86)

LLA(lon=86.925°, lat=27.988°, alt=8848.86m)
All coordinate types are parametric. The type parameter promotes automatically:
LLA(1, 2, 3)        # Int

LLA(lon=1°, lat=2°, alt=3m)
LLA(1.0f0, 2.0f0)   # Float32

LLA(lon=1.0°, lat=2.0°, alt=0.0m)
LLA{Float32}(1, 2)   # Explicit type parameter

LLA(lon=1.0°, lat=2.0°, alt=0.0m)

title: Getting Started
text:
Datums
A Datum defines the shape of the reference ellipsoid. GeodeticOps exports four common datums:

for d in [WGS84, GRS80, CLARKE1866, WGS72]
    println(d)
end

Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)
Datum("GRS 80", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257222101)
Datum("Clarke 1866", a=6.3782064e6, 1/f=294.978698214)
Datum("WGS 72", a=6.378135e6, 1/f=298.26)


All operations default to WGS84. You can override per-call with the datum keyword, or change the global default via DEFAULT_DATUM[]:

# Per-call override
distance(nyc, LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074); datum=GRS80)

5.585233578986238e6



# Change the global default
DEFAULT_DATUM[] = GRS80
DEFAULT_DATUM[]

Datum("GRS 80", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257222101)



# Restore WGS84
DEFAULT_DATUM[] = WGS84

Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)

title: Getting Started
text:
Measuring Distances
Haversine gives a fast great-circle approximation:

london = LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074)
haversine(nyc, london)

5.570229848664454e6


Vincenty is more accurate, accounting for the ellipsoid:

distance(nyc, london)

5.585233578943206e6


The two methods agree to within ~0.5% for most distances.

title: Getting Started
text:
Bearing
Compute the initial bearing (forward azimuth) from one point to another:

bearing(nyc, london)  # degrees clockwise from north

51.24122911946578

title: Getting Started
text:
Computing Destinations
Given a starting point, bearing, and distance, find the destination:

# 1000 km due north from the equator
forward_geodesic(LLA(0.0, 0.0), 0.0, 1_000_000.0)

LLA(lon=0.0°, lat=9.042944436329899°, alt=0.0m)

title: Getting Started
text:
Interpolation Along a Geodesic
Find the point at any fraction along a geodesic path:

tokyo = LLA(139.6917, 35.6895)

midpoint(nyc, tokyo)

LLA(lon=-153.64271957455668°, lat=69.72196141701565°, alt=0.0m)



# Quarter-way point
intermediate_point(nyc, tokyo, 0.25)

LLA(lon=-96.60006370071237°, lat=60.8407105106562°, alt=0.0m)

title: Getting Started
text:
Coordinate Conversions
Convert between any supported coordinate types:

# LLA to ECEF (Earth-Centered, Earth-Fixed)
ecef = ECEF(nyc, WGS84)

ECEF(x=1.333998456149258e6, y=-4.654044842733645e6, z=4.1383002386952764e6)



# ECEF back to LLA
LLA(ecef, WGS84)

LLA(lon=-74.006°, lat=40.7128°, alt=0.0m)



# LLA to UTM
utm = UTM(nyc, WGS84)

UTM(zone=18N, easting=583959.372324113, northing=4.507350998397536e6)


Local coordinate frames (ENU, NED, AER) require a reference point:

# Displacement from NYC to a nearby point in East-North-Up
nearby = LLA(-73.996, 40.722, 50.0)
ENU(nearby, nyc, WGS84)

ENU(e=844.8835733062557, n=1021.7018510246394, u=49.86208985488406)


See Coordinates for the full list of types and conversion paths.

title: Getting Started
text:
Polygon Area
Compute the area of a polygon on the ellipsoid:

# Approximate area of a 1 deg x 1 deg square at the equator
pts = [LLA(0, 0), LLA(1, 0), LLA(1, 1), LLA(0, 1)]
area_km2 = polygon_area(pts) / 1e6
println("$(round(area_km2, digits=0)) km^2")

12364.0 km^2

title: Getting Started
text:
Bounding Boxes
Compute the bounding box of a set of points:

pts = [LLA(-74.0, 40.0), LLA(-73.0, 41.0), LLA(-75.0, 40.5)]
boundingbox(pts)

BoundingBox(lon=[-75.0, -73.0], lat=[40.0, 41.0])


Or find the bounding box of all points within a given distance:

destination_boundingbox(nyc, 50_000.0)  # 50 km radius

BoundingBox(lon=[-74.59771173143642, -73.41428826856358], lat=[40.26252811430199, 41.16303665323765])

title: Coordinates
text:
Type Hierarchy
GeodeticOps provides seven coordinate types organized into a type hierarchy that distinguishes fixed coordinates (those that describe a position on the ellipsoid) from relative coordinates (those measured from a local reference point).
All geodetic operations (distance, bearing, polygon_area, etc.) accept any AbstractFixedCoords. If you pass an ECEF or UTM, it is automatically converted to LLA internally. Passing a relative coordinate type like ENU produces a compile-time method error.

title: Coordinates
text:
AbstractCoords{T}
├── AbstractFixedCoords{T}       # convertible to LLA with only a Datum
│   ├── LLA{T}                   # longitude, latitude (degrees), altitude
│   ├── LLARad{T}                # longitude, latitude (radians), altitude
│   ├── ECEF{T}                  # Earth-Centered, Earth-Fixed (x, y, z)
│   └── UTM{T}                   # Universal Transverse Mercator
└── AbstractRelativeCoords{T}    # require a reference point
    ├── ENU{T}                   # East, North, Up
    ├── NED{T}                   # North, East, Down
    └── AER{T}                   # Azimuth, Elevation, Range

title: Coordinates
text:
Fixed Coordinate Types

LLA – Geodetic Coordinates (Degrees)
The most common type. Longitude and latitude are in degrees, altitude in meters above the ellipsoid.

LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)          # NYC, alt=0

LLA(lon=-74.006°, lat=40.7128°, alt=0.0m)



LLA(86.925, 27.988, 8848.86)   # Everest summit

LLA(lon=86.925°, lat=27.988°, alt=8848.86m)




LLARad – Geodetic Coordinates (Radians)
Same as LLA but angles in radians. Converts to/from LLA without a datum:

r = LLARad(LLA(180.0, 45.0))

LLARad(lon=3.141592653589793 rad, lat=0.7853981633974483 rad, alt=0.0m)



LLA(r)

LLA(lon=180.0°, lat=45.0°, alt=0.0m)




ECEF – Earth-Centered, Earth-Fixed
Cartesian coordinates in meters with origin at the center of the ellipsoid. Requires a datum for conversion:

ecef = ECEF(LLA(0.0, 0.0), WGS84)  # equator / prime meridian

ECEF(x=6.378137e6, y=0.0, z=0.0)



LLA(ecef, WGS84)

LLA(lon=0.0°, lat=0.0°, alt=0.0m)




UTM – Universal Transverse Mercator
Projected coordinates with zone number and hemisphere flag. The zone is auto-detected from longitude:

utm = UTM(LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), WGS84)

UTM(zone=18N, easting=583959.372324113, northing=4.507350998397536e6)



LLA(utm, WGS84)

LLA(lon=-74.00599999999051°, lat=40.71280000050855°, alt=0.0m)


You can force a specific zone:

UTM(LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), WGS84; zone=17)

UTM(zone=17N, easting=1.0909710567325049e6, northing=4.530466275427255e6)

title: Coordinates
text:
Relative Coordinate Types
These describe positions relative to a local reference point. They require both a reference LLA and a Datum for conversion.

ENU – East-North-Up
Local tangent plane with axes pointing east, north, and up:

ref = LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)              # NYC
target = LLA(-73.996, 40.722, 50.0)
enu = ENU(target, ref, WGS84)

ENU(e=844.8835733062557, n=1021.7018510246394, u=49.86208985488406)



# Convert back
LLA(enu, ref, WGS84)

LLA(lon=-73.996°, lat=40.721999999999994°, alt=49.99999999906868m)




NED – North-East-Down
Aviation/aerospace convention. Related to ENU by a simple axis swap:

ned = NED(enu)  # no datum needed, pure axis reordering

NED(n=1021.7018510246394, e=844.8835733062557, d=-49.86208985488406)



ENU(ned)        # round-trip

ENU(e=844.8835733062557, n=1021.7018510246394, u=49.86208985488406)




AER – Azimuth-Elevation-Range
Spherical coordinates relative to a reference point. Converts to/from ENU without a datum:

# Due north, 45 deg elevation, 1000 m range
aer = AER(0.0, 45.0, 1000.0)
ENU(aer)

ENU(e=0.0, n=707.1067811865476, u=707.1067811865474)



# From two LLA points
AER(target, ref, WGS84)

AER(az=39.58862158981313°, el=2.1538513699437423°, range=1326.7212038837015m)

title: Coordinates
text:
Conversion Map
The diagram below shows all supported direct conversions. Arrows indicate constructors; dashed arrows require a datum, solid arrows do not.
LLA ←--→ LLARad          (no datum)
LLA ←--→ ECEF            (datum)
LLA ←--→ UTM             (datum)
LLA ←--→ ENU ←--→ NED    (datum + ref)   (no datum)
LLA ←--→ AER             (datum + ref)
ENU ←--→ AER             (no datum)
ECEF ←--→ ENU            (datum + ref)
ECEF ←--→ UTM            (datum)
Every conversion is a constructor call:

p = LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)

# Chain conversions
p |> x -> ECEF(x, WGS84) |> x -> LLA(x, WGS84)

LLA(lon=-74.006°, lat=40.7128°, alt=0.0m)

title: Coordinates
text:
Visualizing ECEF
The plot below shows three cities converted to ECEF coordinates on a wireframe ellipsoid.

a = 1.0
f_exag = flattening(WGS84) * 50  # exaggerate flattening for visibility
b = a * (1 - f_exag)

theta = range(-pi/2, pi/2, 40)
phi = range(0, 2pi, 80)
X = [a * cos(t) * cos(p) for t in theta, p in phi]
Y = [a * cos(t) * sin(p) for t in theta, p in phi]
Z = [b * sin(t) for t in theta, p in phi]

# Cities in normalized ECEF
cities = [
    ("NYC", LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)),
    ("London", LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074)),
    ("Tokyo", LLA(139.6917, 35.6895)),
]
scale = semi_major_axis(WGS84)
city_ecefs = [(name, ECEF(lla, WGS84)) for (name, lla) in cities]

fig = Figure(size=(600, 500))
ax = Axis3(fig[1, 1], aspect=:data, xlabel="X", ylabel="Y", zlabel="Z")
surface!(ax, X, Y, Z, alpha=0.2, colormap=[:lightblue, :lightblue], shading=NoShading)
wireframe!(ax, X[1:4:end, 1:4:end], Y[1:4:end, 1:4:end], Z[1:4:end, 1:4:end],
    color=(:steelblue, 0.3), linewidth=0.5)

for (name, ecef) in city_ecefs
    px, py, pz = ecef.x / scale, ecef.y / scale, ecef.z / scale
    # Adjust for exaggerated flattening
    pz_exag = pz * (b / (a * (1 - flattening(WGS84))))
    scatter!(ax, [px], [py], [pz_exag], color=:crimson, markersize=12)
    text!(ax, px, py, pz_exag; text=name, fontsize=12, align=(:left, :bottom), offset=(5, 5))
end
fig






Figure 3.1: Three cities plotted in ECEF coordinates on a wireframe ellipsoid (flattening exaggerated 50x for visibility).

title: Geodesics
text:
Distance
A geodesic is the shortest path between two points on the surface of an ellipsoid – the curved-surface equivalent of a straight line. On a flat map (equirectangular projection), geodesics appear as curves because the map distorts the ellipsoid’s geometry.
GeodeticOps provides two distance functions:
nyc = LLA(-74.006, 40.7128)
london = LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074)

h = haversine(nyc, london)
v = distance(nyc, london)

println("Haversine: $(round(h / 1000, digits=1)) km")
println("Vincenty:  $(round(v / 1000, digits=1)) km")
println("Difference: $(round(abs(h - v) / 1000, digits=1)) km ($(round(abs(h - v) / v * 100, digits=2))%)")

Haversine: 5570.2 km
Vincenty:  5585.2 km
Difference: 15.0 km (0.27%)
For most applications, the difference is negligible. Use distance (Vincenty) when sub-kilometer accuracy matters.

title: Geodesics
text:
Function
Method
Accuracy
Speed




haversine
Great-circle on a sphere
~0.5%
Fast


distance
Vincenty on the ellipsoid
~0.5 mm
Iterative

title: Geodesics
text:
Bearing
The bearing (or forward azimuth) is the initial direction of travel, measured in degrees clockwise from north:

bearing(nyc, london)

51.24122911946578


forward_azimuth is an alias for bearing.

title: Geodesics
text:
The Inverse Problem
vincenty_inverse returns the full solution to the inverse geodesic problem – distance plus both azimuths:

vincenty_inverse(nyc, london)

(distance = 5.585233578943206e6, azimuth1 = 51.24122911946578, azimuth2 = 288.3689981132953)


The azimuth2 field is the reverse azimuth at the destination (the direction you’d be facing when you arrive, measured clockwise from north).

title: Geodesics
text:
The Forward Problem
Given a starting point, bearing, and distance, forward_geodesic computes the destination:

# 500 km northeast from NYC
forward_geodesic(nyc, 45.0, 500_000.0)

LLA(lon=-69.61180718365085°, lat=43.81439479626134°, alt=0.0m)


The plot below shows destinations at every 15 degrees of bearing from a point at 45 deg N. Notice the asymmetry: east/west destinations span more longitude than north/south destinations span latitude, because meridians converge toward the poles.

origin = LLA(0.0, 45.0)
dist = 1_500_000.0
bearings_deg = 0:15:345
dests = [forward_geodesic(origin, az, dist) for az in bearings_deg]

fig = Figure(size=(550, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], xlabel="Longitude (deg)", ylabel="Latitude (deg)", aspect=DataAspect())
for d in dests
    lines!(ax, [origin.lon, d.lon], [origin.lat, d.lat], color=(:steelblue, 0.4), linewidth=1)
end
scatter!(ax, [d.lon for d in dests], [d.lat for d in dests], color=:steelblue, markersize=8)
scatter!(ax, [origin.lon], [origin.lat], color=:crimson, markersize=14)
text!(ax, origin.lon + 0.5, origin.lat + 0.5; text="Origin", fontsize=12, align=(:left, :bottom))

# Label cardinal directions
for (az, label) in [(0, "N"), (90, "E"), (180, "S"), (270, "W")]
    d = forward_geodesic(origin, Float64(az), dist)
    text!(ax, d.lon, d.lat; text=label, fontsize=13, align=(:center, :center),
        color=:gray30, offset=(0, 12))
end
fig






Figure 4.1: Destinations 1500 km from 45 deg N, 0 deg E at bearings 0 deg through 345 deg. The asymmetry reflects meridian convergence at higher latitudes.

title: Geodesics
text:
Interpolation
intermediate_point returns the point at a given fraction along the geodesic. midpoint is a shortcut for fraction = 0.5.

tokyo = LLA(139.6917, 35.6895)

midpoint(nyc, tokyo)

LLA(lon=-153.64271957455668°, lat=69.72196141701565°, alt=0.0m)



# Points at 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%
for f in 0.0:0.25:1.0
    p = intermediate_point(nyc, london, f)
    println("f=$f: $p")
end

f=0.0: LLA(lon=-74.006°, lat=40.7128°, alt=0.0m)
f=0.25: LLA(lon=-59.4518518641905°, lat=47.72478882322425°, alt=0.0m)
f=0.5: LLA(lon=-41.27790142901274°, lat=52.3894744727656°, alt=0.0m)
f=0.75: LLA(lon=-20.491869592369756°, lat=53.77005735563994°, alt=0.0m)
f=1.0: LLA(lon=-0.12780000011453324°, lat=51.507400000012495°, alt=0.0m)



fracs = range(0, 1, 20)
pts = [intermediate_point(nyc, london, f) for f in fracs]
lons = [p.lon for p in pts]
lats = [p.lat for p in pts]

fig = Figure(size=(700, 350))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], xlabel="Longitude (deg)", ylabel="Latitude (deg)")
scatter!(ax, lons, lats, color=collect(fracs), colormap=:viridis, markersize=10)
Colorbar(fig[1, 2], colormap=:viridis, limits=(0, 1), label="Fraction")
text!(ax, lons[1] - 1, lats[1] - 1.5; text="NYC", fontsize=13, align=(:center, :top))
text!(ax, lons[end] + 1.5, lats[end] + 0.5; text="London", fontsize=13, align=(:left, :bottom))
fig






Figure 4.2: Twenty evenly-spaced intermediate points along the geodesic from New York City to London. Color indicates fraction along the path.

title: Geodesics
text:
Geodesic vs. Rhumb Line
A rhumb line (loxodrome) crosses all meridians at the same angle – it appears as a straight line on a Mercator map. A geodesic is shorter but changes bearing continuously.

pairs = [
    (LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074), "NYC", "London"),
    (LLA(-122.4194, 37.7749), LLA(139.6917, 35.6895), "SF", "Tokyo"),
    (LLA(-43.1729, -22.9068), LLA(18.4241, -33.9249), "Rio", "Cape Town"),
]

fig = Figure(size=(700, 700))
for (i, (a, b, name_a, name_b)) in enumerate(pairs)
    ax = Axis(fig[i, 1], xlabel= i == 3 ? "Longitude (deg)" : "", ylabel="Latitude (deg)",
        title="$name_a to $name_b")

    # Geodesic
    n = 100
    geo = [intermediate_point(a, b, f) for f in range(0, 1, n)]
    lines!(ax, [p.lon for p in geo], [p.lat for p in geo], color=:steelblue, linewidth=2.5, label="Geodesic")

    # Rhumb line
    lines!(ax, collect(range(a.lon, b.lon, n)), collect(range(a.lat, b.lat, n)),
        color=:gray60, linewidth=1.5, linestyle=:dash, label="Rhumb line")

    scatter!(ax, [a.lon, b.lon], [a.lat, b.lat], color=:crimson, markersize=10)
    text!(ax, a.lon, a.lat; text=name_a, fontsize=11, offset=(0, -12), align=(:center, :top))
    text!(ax, b.lon, b.lat; text=name_b, fontsize=11, offset=(0, 8), align=(:center, :bottom))

    i == 1 && axislegend(ax, position=:rb)
end
fig






Figure 4.3: Geodesic (blue) versus rhumb line (gray dashed) for three city pairs. The geodesic always curves toward the nearer pole.




Note: The San Francisco to Tokyo geodesic curves far northward over the Pacific – this is why transpacific flights follow polar routes.

title: Areas and Bounding Boxes
text:
Polygon Area
polygon_area computes the area of a polygon on the ellipsoid in square meters. The polygon is defined by an ordered sequence of vertices and is implicitly closed (the last vertex connects back to the first).
The algorithm uses the spherical excess method on the authalic (equal-area) sphere – a sphere with the same surface area as the ellipsoid.
# 1 deg x 1 deg square at the equator
square = [LLA(0, 0), LLA(1, 0), LLA(1, 1), LLA(0, 1)]
area = polygon_area(square)
println("Area: $(round(area / 1e6, digits=0)) km^2")

Area: 12364.0 km^2
# A larger triangle
triangle = [LLA(-10, 0), LLA(10, 0), LLA(0, 15)]
area = polygon_area(triangle)
println("Area: $(round(area / 1e6, digits=0)) km^2")

Area: 1.833537e6 km^2
Vertex order matters for the sign of the spherical excess, but polygon_area returns the absolute value, so either winding order gives the same result:
polygon_area(reverse(square)) == polygon_area(square)

true
polygons = [
    ("Equatorial square", [LLA(0, 0), LLA(5, 0), LLA(5, 5), LLA(0, 5)]),
    ("Mid-latitude square", [LLA(0, 40), LLA(5, 40), LLA(5, 45), LLA(0, 45)]),
    ("Triangle", [LLA(-5, 20), LLA(5, 20), LLA(0, 30)]),
]
colors = [:steelblue, :seagreen, :coral]

fig = Figure(size=(700, 400))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1], xlabel="Longitude (deg)", ylabel="Latitude (deg)")
for (i, (label, verts)) in enumerate(polygons)
    lons = [v.lon for v in verts]
    lats = [v.lat for v in verts]
    # Close the polygon for plotting
    push!(lons, lons[1])
    push!(lats, lats[1])

    poly!(ax, Point2f.(lons, lats), color=(colors[i], 0.3), strokecolor=colors[i], strokewidth=2)

    area_km2 = round(polygon_area(verts) / 1e6, digits=0)
    cx = sum(v.lon for v in verts) / length(verts)
    cy = sum(v.lat for v in verts) / length(verts)
    text!(ax, cx, cy; text="$(Int(area_km2)) km^2", fontsize=11, align=(:center, :center), color=colors[i])
end
fig






Figure 5.1: Three polygons with their computed areas. Polygon area varies with latitude even for the same angular extent.
Notice that the mid-latitude square has a smaller area than the equatorial square of the same angular extent – this reflects the convergence of meridians at higher latitudes.

title: Areas and Bounding Boxes
text:
Bounding Boxes
A BoundingBox is an axis-aligned rectangle in longitude/latitude space:

bb = BoundingBox(-75.0, 40.0, -73.0, 41.0)

BoundingBox(lon=[-75.0, -73.0], lat=[40.0, 41.0])



From Points
boundingbox computes the smallest bounding box containing a set of points:

pts = [LLA(-74.0, 40.0), LLA(-73.5, 40.8), LLA(-74.5, 40.3), LLA(-73.2, 40.5)]
bb = boundingbox(pts)

BoundingBox(lon=[-74.5, -73.2], lat=[40.0, 40.8])




Containment
Test whether a point lies within a bounding box:

in(LLA(-74.0, 40.5), bb)

true



in(LLA(-72.0, 40.5), bb)

false


The in function handles antimeridian wrapping automatically. If min_lon > max_lon, the box is assumed to wrap across the antimeridian.


Destination Bounding Box
destination_boundingbox computes the bounding box of all points within a given distance from an origin. This is useful for spatial queries (“find all records within 50 km”):

destination_boundingbox(LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), 50_000.0)

BoundingBox(lon=[-74.59771173143642, -73.41428826856358], lat=[40.26252811430199, 41.16303665323765])



fig = Figure(size=(700, 350))

# Left: bounding box from points
pts = [
    LLA(-74.0, 40.0), LLA(-73.5, 40.8), LLA(-74.5, 40.3),
    LLA(-73.2, 40.5), LLA(-74.2, 40.9), LLA(-73.8, 40.1),
]
bb = boundingbox(pts)
ax1 = Axis(fig[1, 1], xlabel="Longitude (deg)", ylabel="Latitude (deg)", title="Bounding Box")
scatter!(ax1, [p.lon for p in pts], [p.lat for p in pts], color=:steelblue, markersize=10)
box_lons = [bb.min_lon, bb.max_lon, bb.max_lon, bb.min_lon, bb.min_lon]
box_lats = [bb.min_lat, bb.min_lat, bb.max_lat, bb.max_lat, bb.min_lat]
lines!(ax1, box_lons, box_lats, color=:crimson, linewidth=2)

# Right: destination bounding box
origin = LLA(0.0, 45.0)
dist = 500_000.0
dbb = destination_boundingbox(origin, dist)
ax2 = Axis(fig[1, 2], xlabel="Longitude (deg)", ylabel="Latitude (deg)", title="Destination Bounding Box")

# Draw the "circle" of destinations
ring = [forward_geodesic(origin, Float64(az), dist) for az in 0:5:360]
poly!(ax2, Point2f.([p.lon for p in ring], [p.lat for p in ring]),
    color=(:steelblue, 0.15), strokecolor=:steelblue, strokewidth=1.5)

# Draw the bounding box
dbox_lons = [dbb.min_lon, dbb.max_lon, dbb.max_lon, dbb.min_lon, dbb.min_lon]
dbox_lats = [dbb.min_lat, dbb.min_lat, dbb.max_lat, dbb.max_lat, dbb.min_lat]
lines!(ax2, dbox_lons, dbox_lats, color=:crimson, linewidth=2)
scatter!(ax2, [origin.lon], [origin.lat], color=:crimson, markersize=10)
text!(ax2, origin.lon + 0.3, origin.lat + 0.3; text="Origin", fontsize=11, align=(:left, :bottom))

fig






Figure 5.2: A bounding box around scattered points (left) and a destination bounding box showing the area within 500 km of a central point (right).






Accepts Any AbstractFixedCoords
All functions accept any AbstractFixedCoords type, not just LLA. ECEF and UTM coordinates are converted automatically:

ecef_pts = [ECEF(p, WGS84) for p in pts]
boundingbox(ecef_pts)

BoundingBox(lon=[-74.5, -73.2], lat=[40.00000000000001, 40.9])

title: API
text: Types

title: API
text:
AER


AER{T}(az, el, range)
Azimuth-Elevation-Range coordinates relative to a reference point.

Fields

az::T: Azimuth in degrees, clockwise from north [0, 360).
el::T: Elevation in degrees, up from horizon [−90, 90].
range::T: Slant range in meters.

AER(c::ENU)
Convert ENU to AER coordinates (no datum needed — pure geometry).
AER(c::LLA, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert LLA to AER relative to the reference point ref.
AER(c::ECEF, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert ECEF to AER relative to the reference point ref.





AbstractCoords


AbstractCoords{T}
Abstract supertype for all coordinate representations.




AbstractFixedCoords


AbstractFixedCoords{T} <: AbstractCoords{T}
Abstract supertype for coordinate types that represent a fixed position on the ellipsoid and can be converted to LLA using only a Datum (no reference point required).
Subtypes: LLA, LLARad, ECEF, UTM.




AbstractRelativeCoords


AbstractRelativeCoords{T} <: AbstractCoords{T}
Abstract supertype for coordinate types that are relative to a reference point. These require both a Datum and a reference LLA for conversion.
Subtypes: ENU, NED, AER.




BoundingBox


BoundingBox(min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat)
An axis-aligned bounding box in geodetic coordinates (degrees).
When min_lon > max_lon, the box wraps across the antimeridian.

Examples
julia> bbox = BoundingBox(-75.0, 40.0, -73.0, 41.0)
BoundingBox(lon=[-75.0, -73.0], lat=[40.0, 41.0])





Datum


Datum(name, a, f_inv)
A geodetic datum defined by an ellipsoid of revolution.

Fields

name::String: Human-readable name (e.g. "WGS 84").
a::Float64: Semi-major axis in meters (equatorial radius).
f_inv::Float64: Inverse flattening (1/f). Use Inf for a perfect sphere.


Examples
julia> WGS84
Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)

julia> semi_major_axis(WGS84)
6.378137e6

julia> semi_minor_axis(WGS84)
6.356752314245179e6






ECEF


ECEF{T}(x, y, z)
Earth-Centered, Earth-Fixed Cartesian coordinates in meters.

Fields

x::T: X-axis (intersection of prime meridian and equator).
y::T: Y-axis (90° east on the equator).
z::T: Z-axis (north pole).


Examples
julia> ECEF(6_378_137.0, 0.0, 0.0)
ECEF(x=6.378137e6, y=0.0, z=0.0)
ECEF(c::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert geodetic coordinates (degrees) to ECEF Cartesian coordinates.
ECEF(c::LLARad, datum::Datum)
Convert geodetic coordinates (radians) to ECEF Cartesian coordinates.
ECEF(c::ENU, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert ENU coordinates back to ECEF given the reference point ref.
ECEF(c::NED, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert NED coordinates back to ECEF given the reference point ref.
ECEF(c::AER, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert AER to ECEF given the reference point ref.






ENU


ENU{T}(e, n, u)
East-North-Up local tangent plane coordinates in meters, relative to a reference point.

Fields

e::T: East displacement in meters.
n::T: North displacement in meters.
u::T: Up displacement in meters.

ENU(c::ECEF, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert ECEF coordinates to ENU relative to the reference point ref.
ENU(c::LLA, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert LLA coordinates to ENU relative to the reference point ref.
ENU(c::AER)
Convert AER to ENU coordinates (no datum needed — pure geometry).





LLA


LLA{T}(lon, lat, alt=0)
Geodetic coordinates: longitude, latitude (degrees), and altitude (meters above ellipsoid).

Fields

lon::T: Longitude in degrees (−180, 180].
lat::T: Latitude in degrees [−90, 90].
alt::T: Altitude above the ellipsoid in meters.


Examples
julia> LLA(0.0, 0.0)
LLA(lon=0.0°, lat=0.0°, alt=0.0m)

julia> LLA{Float32}(1, 2, 3)
LLA(lon=1.0°, lat=2.0°, alt=3.0m)
LLA(c::ECEF, datum::Datum)
Convert ECEF Cartesian coordinates to geodetic coordinates (degrees). Uses Bowring’s iterative method.
LLA(c::ENU, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert ENU coordinates to LLA given the reference point ref.
LLA(c::NED, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert NED coordinates to LLA given the reference point ref.
LLA(c::AER, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert AER to LLA given the reference point ref.
LLA(c::UTM, datum::Datum)
Convert UTM to LLA.






LLARad


LLARad{T}(lon, lat, alt=0)
Geodetic coordinates: longitude, latitude (radians), and altitude (meters above ellipsoid).

Fields

lon::T: Longitude in radians (−π, π].
lat::T: Latitude in radians [−π/2, π/2].
alt::T: Altitude above the ellipsoid in meters.


Examples
julia> LLARad(0.0, 0.0)
LLARad(lon=0.0 rad, lat=0.0 rad, alt=0.0m)

julia> LLARad(LLA(180.0, 45.0))
LLARad(lon=3.141592653589793 rad, lat=0.7853981633974483 rad, alt=0.0m)
LLARad(c::ECEF, datum::Datum)
Convert ECEF Cartesian coordinates to geodetic coordinates (radians).






NED


NED{T}(n, e, d)
North-East-Down local tangent plane coordinates in meters, relative to a reference point. Standard aviation/aerospace convention.

Fields

n::T: North displacement in meters.
e::T: East displacement in meters.
d::T: Down displacement in meters.

NED(c::ECEF, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert ECEF coordinates to NED relative to the reference point ref.
NED(c::LLA, ref::LLA, datum::Datum)
Convert LLA coordinates to NED relative to the reference point ref.





UTM


UTM{T}(zone, isnorth, easting, northing)
Universal Transverse Mercator projected coordinates.

Fields

zone::Int: UTM zone number (1–60).
isnorth::Bool: true for northern hemisphere, false for southern.
easting::T: Easting in meters.
northing::T: Northing in meters.

!!! note Standard zone determination only. Norway/Svalbard exceptions (zones 32V, 31X–37X) are not handled.
UTM(c::LLA, datum::Datum; zone=nothing)
Convert LLA to UTM. Zone is auto-detected from longitude unless specified.

title: API
text:
Functions


bearing


forward_azimuth(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> Float64
Compute the forward azimuth (degrees, clockwise from north) from a to b.

Examples
julia> forward_azimuth(LLA(0.0, 0.0), LLA(1.0, 1.0))
44.99563948...





boundingbox


boundingbox(points; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> BoundingBox
Compute the axis-aligned bounding box for a collection of AbstractFixedCoords.

Examples
julia> pts = [LLA(-74.0, 40.0), LLA(-73.0, 41.0), LLA(-75.0, 40.5)];

julia> boundingbox(pts)
BoundingBox(lon=[-75.0, -73.0], lat=[40.0, 41.0])





destination_boundingbox


destination_boundingbox(origin::AbstractFixedCoords, dist; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> BoundingBox
Compute the bounding box of all points within dist meters of origin.

Examples
julia> destination_boundingbox(LLA(0.0, 0.0), 100_000.0)
BoundingBox(lon=[-0.9, 0.9], lat=[-0.9, 0.9])





distance


distance(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> Float64
Compute the geodesic distance in meters between two points using Vincenty’s formula.

Examples
julia> distance(LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074))
5.5702e6





eccentricity


eccentricity(d::Datum) -> Float64
Return the first eccentricity: e = √(2f - f²).




eccentricity_sq


eccentricity_sq(d::Datum) -> Float64
Return the first eccentricity squared: e² = 2f - f².




flattening


flattening(d::Datum) -> Float64
Return the flattening f = 1 / f_inv.




forward_azimuth


forward_azimuth(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> Float64
Compute the forward azimuth (degrees, clockwise from north) from a to b.

Examples
julia> forward_azimuth(LLA(0.0, 0.0), LLA(1.0, 1.0))
44.99563948...





forward_geodesic


forward_geodesic(origin::AbstractFixedCoords, azimuth, dist; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> LLA
Solve the forward (direct) geodesic problem: given a starting point, an azimuth (degrees, clockwise from north), and a distance (meters), compute the destination point.

Examples
julia> forward_geodesic(LLA(0.0, 0.0), 90.0, 111_320.0)
LLA(lon=1.0°, lat=0.0°, alt=0.0m)





haversine


haversine(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> Float64
Compute the great-circle distance in meters between two coordinates using the haversine formula. Uses the mean radius R = (2a + b) / 3 of the ellipsoid defined by datum. Altitude is ignored.
Accepts any AbstractFixedCoords (LLA, LLARad, ECEF, UTM).

Examples
julia> haversine(LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074))  # ≈ 5570 km
5.570222e6





intermediate_point


intermediate_point(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords, fraction; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> LLA
Return the point at the given fraction (0 to 1) along the geodesic from a to b.

Examples
julia> intermediate_point(LLA(0.0, 0.0), LLA(10.0, 0.0), 0.5)
LLA(lon=5.0°, lat=0.0°, alt=0.0m)





midpoint


midpoint(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> LLA
Return the midpoint along the geodesic from a to b.

Examples
julia> midpoint(LLA(0.0, 0.0), LLA(10.0, 0.0))
LLA(lon=5.0°, lat=0.0°, alt=0.0m)





polygon_area


polygon_area(points; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[]) -> Float64
Compute the area of a polygon on the ellipsoid in square meters. The polygon is defined by an ordered sequence of AbstractFixedCoords vertices. The polygon is implicitly closed (the last vertex connects back to the first).
Uses the spherical excess method with the authalic (equal-area) sphere radius.

Examples
julia> # Approximate area of a 1°×1° square at the equator (~12,300 km²)
       pts = [LLA(0, 0), LLA(1, 0), LLA(1, 1), LLA(0, 1)];

julia> polygon_area(pts) / 1e6  # km²
≈ 12308





semi_major_axis


semi_major_axis(d::Datum) -> Float64
Return the semi-major axis (equatorial radius) in meters.




semi_minor_axis


semi_minor_axis(d::Datum) -> Float64
Return the semi-minor axis (polar radius) in meters: b = a * (1 - f).




vincenty_inverse


vincenty_inverse(a::AbstractFixedCoords, b::AbstractFixedCoords; datum=DEFAULT_DATUM[])
Solve the inverse geodesic problem using Vincenty’s iterative method. Returns a NamedTuple with:

distance: geodesic distance in meters.
azimuth1: forward azimuth at a (degrees, clockwise from north).
azimuth2: reverse azimuth at b (degrees, clockwise from north).

Accepts any AbstractFixedCoords (LLA, LLARad, ECEF, UTM).

Examples
julia> vincenty_inverse(LLA(-74.006, 40.7128), LLA(-0.1278, 51.5074))
(distance = 5.5702e6, azimuth1 = 51.2, azimuth2 = 107.9)

title: API
text:
Constants


CLARKE1866


Datum(name, a, f_inv)
A geodetic datum defined by an ellipsoid of revolution.

Fields

name::String: Human-readable name (e.g. "WGS 84").
a::Float64: Semi-major axis in meters (equatorial radius).
f_inv::Float64: Inverse flattening (1/f). Use Inf for a perfect sphere.


Examples
julia> WGS84
Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)

julia> semi_major_axis(WGS84)
6.378137e6

julia> semi_minor_axis(WGS84)
6.356752314245179e6






DEFAULT_DATUM


No documentation found for private binding Base.RefValue.

Summary
mutable struct Base.RefValue{T}


Fields
x :: T


Supertype Hierarchy
Base.RefValue{T} <: Ref{T} <: Any





GRS80


Datum(name, a, f_inv)
A geodetic datum defined by an ellipsoid of revolution.

Fields

name::String: Human-readable name (e.g. "WGS 84").
a::Float64: Semi-major axis in meters (equatorial radius).
f_inv::Float64: Inverse flattening (1/f). Use Inf for a perfect sphere.


Examples
julia> WGS84
Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)

julia> semi_major_axis(WGS84)
6.378137e6

julia> semi_minor_axis(WGS84)
6.356752314245179e6






WGS72


Datum(name, a, f_inv)
A geodetic datum defined by an ellipsoid of revolution.

Fields

name::String: Human-readable name (e.g. "WGS 84").
a::Float64: Semi-major axis in meters (equatorial radius).
f_inv::Float64: Inverse flattening (1/f). Use Inf for a perfect sphere.


Examples
julia> WGS84
Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)

julia> semi_major_axis(WGS84)
6.378137e6

julia> semi_minor_axis(WGS84)
6.356752314245179e6






WGS84


Datum(name, a, f_inv)
A geodetic datum defined by an ellipsoid of revolution.

Fields

name::String: Human-readable name (e.g. "WGS 84").
a::Float64: Semi-major axis in meters (equatorial radius).
f_inv::Float64: Inverse flattening (1/f). Use Inf for a perfect sphere.


Examples
julia> WGS84
Datum("WGS 84", a=6.378137e6, 1/f=298.257223563)

julia> semi_major_axis(WGS84)
6.378137e6

julia> semi_minor_axis(WGS84)

text: Unreleased
